evidence_basis_summary: Full text is scoped to disclosure of AI use in authoring; no treatment of how materials or cases enter an analysis universe.
notes: Upgraded to high after full-text read confirms scope does not touch relevance adjudication.

evidence_basis_summary: Paper concerns reporting of AI-assisted research/writing activities; it does not constrain causal or mechanism claims.
notes: Upgraded to high after full-text read; disclosure tool is not about mechanism discipline.

evidence_basis_summary: DAISY fixes six activity categories for reporting AI use but does not set constructs or preserve perspective disagreement in a substantive study.
notes: Upgraded to high; category design is for disclosure scaffolding not construct adjudication.

evidence_basis_summary: No treatment of corpus breadth or machine-legible corpus collapse; the study is a user study of a disclosure form.
notes: Upgraded to high based on full-text scope check.

evidence_basis_summary: DAISY explicitly designs a disclosure workflow with content (R1), presentation (R2), and context-of-use (R3) requirements covering tool names, versions, affected manuscript parts, safeguards, human-review steps, and authorship boundaries.
notes: Upgraded to high and status shifted from merge_pressure to robust: paper is a direct, detailed case of humans architecting disclosure/provenance protocol.

evidence_basis_summary: Paper does not address whether results travel across populations or settings; scope is author disclosure experience.
notes: Upgraded to high after full-text scope confirmation.

evidence_basis_summary: Co-design and user study involve interviews, but the paper does not theorise the interviewer's situated presence as changing what data exist in the studied research itself.
notes: Upgraded to high; method uses interviews but not as the analytic role under study.

evidence_basis_summary: DAISY's design explicitly encodes authorship boundaries and affirms that authors retain full responsibility for final content, and the paper frames disclosure as supporting accountability and attribution norms.
notes: Upgraded to high and status shifted from merge_pressure to robust: disclosure is framed directly as an accountability-and-attribution allocator, not merely as protocol.

evidence_basis_summary: No coding or category-boundary task on a substantive construct; DAISY's six activity categories are disclosure scaffolding.
notes: Upgraded to high after full-text read.

evidence_basis_summary: No preservation of subgroup disagreement in coding; disciplinary contrast (HCI vs non-HCI) is reported descriptively but not as a sampling role.
notes: Upgraded to high; disciplinary differences are findings not a pluralism role.

evidence_basis_summary: No transportability or counterfactual claim is pressured in the paper.
notes: Upgraded to high after full-text read.

evidence_basis_summary: No benchmarking or simulator-calibration work; the user study measures completeness of disclosures not model calibration.
notes: Upgraded to high after full-text read.

evidence_basis_summary: Co-design sessions and surveys are used as method but not analysed as a situated-interlocutor role shaping what respondents disclose.
notes: Upgraded to high; method artefact not analytic role in the case.

evidence_basis_summary: No tacit/archival interpretive work is required to read traces; the study relies on structured self-report.
notes: Upgraded to high after full-text read.

evidence_basis_summary: No title/abstract triage or early ranking task is present; the paper does not delegate screening to AI.
notes: Upgraded to high after full-text read.

role_label: Routine coder on high-consensus, ground-truth-rich tasks
evidence_basis_summary: Thematic analysis of co-design transcripts is done inductively by the first author with team discussion; no high-consensus ground-truth coding cluster is delegated.
notes: Upgraded to high after full-text read.

evidence_basis_summary: Paper explicitly treats Writing and drafting and Language editing and proofreading as AI-assisted activities that authors must disclose, and cites survey evidence that over 80% of researchers use such AI assistance.
notes: Upgraded to high, status kept conditional: prose polishing is openly acknowledged as shrinkable human work, but the whole paper argues it survives only under disclosure/accountability conditions.
